tools.name: create_directory
tools.description: Create a new directory structure in the workspace. Will recursively create all directories in the path, like mkdir -p. You do not need to use this tool before using create_file, that tool will automatically create the needed directories.

dirPath.description: The absolute path to the directory to create.

tools.name: create_file
tools.description: This is a tool for creating a new file in the workspace. The file will be created with the specified content. The directory will be created if it does not already exist. Never use this tool to edit a file that already exists.

filePath.description: The absolute path to the file to create.

content.description: The content to write to the file.

tools.name: create_new_jupyter_notebook
tools.description: Generates a new Jupyter Notebook (.ipynb) in VS Code. Jupyter Notebooks are interactive documents commonly used for data exploration, analysis, visualization, and combining code with narrative text. Prefer creating plain Python files or similar unless a user explicitly requests creating a new Jupyter Notebook or already has a Jupyter Notebook opened or exists in the workspace.

query.description: The query to use to generate the jupyter notebook. This should be a clear and concise description of the notebook the user wants to create.

tools.name: create_new_workspace
tools.description:
Get comprehensive setup steps to help the user create complete project structures in a VS Code workspace. This tool is designed for full project initialization and scaffolding, not for creating individual files.

When to use this tool:
- User wants to create a new complete project from scratch
- Setting up entire project frameworks (TypeScript projects, React apps, Node.js servers, etc.)
- Initializing Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers with full structure
- Creating VS Code extensions with proper scaffolding
- Setting up Next.js, Vite, or other framework-based projects
- User asks for "new project", "create a workspace", "set up a [framework] project"
- Need to establish complete development environment with dependencies, config files, and folder structure

When NOT to use this tool:
- Creating single files or small code snippets
- Adding individual files to existing projects
- Making modifications to existing codebases
- User asks to "create a file" or "add a component"
- Simple code examples or demonstrations
- Debugging or fixing existing code

This tool provides complete project setup including:
- Folder structure creation
- Package.json and dependency management
- Configuration files (tsconfig, eslint, etc.)
- Initial boilerplate code
- Development environment setup
- Build and run instructions

Use other file creation tools for individual files within existing projects.

query.description: The query to use to generate the new workspace. This should be a clear and concise description of the workspace the user wants to create.

tools.name: edit_notebook_file
tools.description:
This is a tool for editing an existing Notebook file in the workspace. Generate the "explanation" property first.
The system is very smart and can understand how to apply your edits to the notebooks.
When updating the content of an existing cell, ensure newCode preserves whitespace and indentation exactly and does NOT include any code markers such as (...existing code...).

filePath.description: An absolute path to the notebook file to edit, or the URI of a untitled, not yet named, file, such as `untitled:Untitled-1.

cellId.description: Id of the cell that needs to be deleted or edited. Use the value `TOP`, `BOTTOM` when inserting a cell at the top or bottom of the notebook, else provide the id of the cell after which a new cell is to be inserted. Remember, if a cellId is provided and editType=insert, then a cell will be inserted after the cell with the provided cellId.

anyOf.description: The code for the new or existing cell to be edited. Code should not be wrapped within <VSCode.Cell> tags. Do NOT include code markers such as (...existing code...) to indicate existing code.

items.description: The code for the new or existing cell to be edited. Code should not be wrapped within <VSCode.Cell> tags

language.description: The language of the cell. `markdown`, `python`, `javascript`, `julia`, etc.

editType.description:
The operation peformed on the cell, whether `insert`, `delete` or `edit`.
Use the `editType` field to specify the operation: `insert` to add a new cell, `edit` to modify an existing cell's content, and `delete` to remove a cell.

tools.name: fetch_webpage
tools.description: Fetches the main content from a web page. This tool is useful for summarizing or analyzing the content of a webpage. You should use this tool when you think the user is looking for information from a specific webpage.

urls.description: An array of URLs to fetch content from.

query.description: The query to search for in the web page's content. This should be a clear and concise description of the content you want to find.

tools.name: file_search
tools.description:
Search for files in the workspace by glob pattern. This only returns the paths of matching files. Use this tool when you know the exact filename pattern of the files you're searching for. Glob patterns match from the root of the workspace folder. Examples:
- **/*.{js,ts} to match all js/ts files in the workspace.
- src/** to match all files under the top-level src folder.
- **/foo/**/*.js to match all js files under any foo folder in the workspace.

query.description: Search for files with names or paths matching this glob pattern.

maxResults.description: The maximum number of results to return. Do not use this unless necessary, it can slow things down. By default, only some matches are returned. If you use this and don't see what you're looking for, you can try again with a more specific query or a larger maxResults.

tools.name: grep_search
tools.description: Do a fast text search in the workspace. Use this tool when you want to search with an exact string or regex. If you are not sure what words will appear in the workspace, prefer using regex patterns with alternation (|) or character classes to search for multiple potential words at once instead of making separate searches. For example, use 'function|method|procedure' to look for all of those words at once. Use includePattern to search within files matching a specific pattern, or in a specific file, using a relative path. Use 'includeIgnoredFiles' to include files normally ignored by .gitignore, other ignore files, and `files.exclude` and `search.exclude` settings. Warning: using this may cause the search to be slower, only set it when you want to search in ignored folders like node_modules or build outputs. Use this tool when you want to see an overview of a particular file, instead of using read_file many times to look for code within a file.

query.description: The pattern to search for in files in the workspace. Use regex with alternation (e.g., 'word1|word2|word3') or character classes to find multiple potential words in a single search. Be sure to set the isRegexp property properly to declare whether it's a regex or plain text pattern. Is case-insensitive.

isRegexp.description: Whether the pattern is a regex.

includePattern.description: Search files matching this glob pattern. Will be applied to the relative path of files within the workspace. To search recursively inside a folder, use a proper glob pattern like "src/folder/**". Do not use | in includePattern.

maxResults.description: The maximum number of results to return. Do not use this unless necessary, it can slow things down. By default, only some matches are returned. If you use this and don't see what you're looking for, you can try again with a more specific query or a larger maxResults.

includeIgnoredFiles.description: Whether to include files that would normally be ignored according to .gitignore, other ignore files and `files.exclude` and `search.exclude` settings. Warning: using this may cause the search to be slower. Only set it when you want to search in ignored folders like node_modules or build outputs.

tools.name: get_changed_files
tools.description: Get git diffs of current file changes in a git repository. Don't forget that you can use run_in_terminal to run git commands in a terminal as well.

repositoryPath.description: The absolute path to the git repository to look for changes in. If not provided, the active git repository will be used.

sourceControlState.description: The kinds of git state to filter by. Allowed values are: 'staged', 'unstaged', and 'merge-conflicts'. If not provided, all states will be included.

tools.name: get_errors
tools.description: Get any compile or lint errors in a specific file or across all files. If the user mentions errors or problems in a file, they may be referring to these. Use the tool to see the same errors that the user is seeing. If the user asks you to analyze all errors, or does not specify a file, use this tool to gather errors for all files. Also use this tool after editing a file to validate the change.

filePaths.description: The absolute paths to the files or folders to check for errors. Omit 'filePaths' when retrieving all errors.

tools.name: copilot_getNotebookSummary
tools.description: This is a tool returns the list of the Notebook cells along with the id, cell types, line ranges, language, execution information and output mime types for each cell. This is useful to get Cell Ids when executing a notebook or determine what cells have been executed and what order, or what cells have outputs. If required to read contents of a cell use this to determine the line range of a cells, and then use read_file tool to read a specific line range. Requery this tool if the contents of the notebook change.

filePath.description: An absolute path to the notebook file with the cell to run, or the URI of a untitled, not yet named, file, such as `untitled:Untitled-1.ipynb

tools.name: get_project_setup_info
tools.description: Do not call this tool without first calling the tool to create a workspace. This tool provides a project setup information for a Visual Studio Code workspace based on a project type and programming language.

projectType.description: The type of project to create. Supported values are: 'python-script', 'python-project', 'mcp-server', 'model-context-protocol-server', 'vscode-extension', 'next-js', 'vite' and 'other'

tools.name: get_search_view_results
tools.description: The results from the search view

tools.name: get_vscode_api
tools.description:
Get comprehensive VS Code API documentation and references for extension development. This tool provides authoritative documentation for VS Code's extensive API surface, including proposed APIs, contribution points, and best practices. Use this tool for understanding complex VS Code API interactions.

When to use this tool:
- User asks about specific VS Code APIs, interfaces, or extension capabilities
- Need documentation for VS Code extension contribution points (commands, views, settings, etc.)
- Questions about proposed APIs and their usage patterns
- Understanding VS Code extension lifecycle, activation events, and packaging
- Best practices for VS Code extension development architecture
- API examples and code patterns for extension features
- Troubleshooting extension-specific issues or API limitations

When NOT to use this tool:
- Creating simple standalone files or scripts unrelated to VS Code extensions
- General programming questions not specific to VS Code extension development
- Questions about using VS Code as an editor (user-facing features)
- Non-extension related development tasks
- File creation or editing that doesn't involve VS Code extension APIs

CRITICAL usage guidelines:
1. Always include specific API names, interfaces, or concepts in your query
2. Mention the extension feature you're trying to implement
3. Include context about proposed vs stable APIs when relevant
4. Reference specific contribution points when asking about extension manifest
5. Be specific about the VS Code version or API version when known

Scope: This tool is for EXTENSION DEVELOPMENT ONLY - building tools that extend VS Code itself, not for general file creation or non-extension programming tasks.

query.description: The query to search vscode documentation for. Should contain all relevant context.

tools.name: github_repo
tools.description: Searches a GitHub repository for relevant source code snippets. Only use this tool if the user is very clearly asking for code snippets from a specific GitHub repository. Do not use this tool for Github repos that the user has open in their workspace.

repo.description: The name of the Github repository to search for code in. Should must be formatted as '<owner>/<repo>'.

query.description: The query to search for repo. Should contain all relevant context.

tools.name: install_extension
tools.description: Install an extension in VS Code. Use this tool to install an extension in Visual Studio Code as part of a new workspace creation process only.

id.description: The ID of the extension to install. This should be in the format <publisher>.<extension>.

name.description: The name of the extension to install. This should be a clear and concise description of the extension.

tools.name: list_dir
tools.description: List the contents of a directory. Result will have the name of the child. If the name ends in /, it's a folder, otherwise a file

path.description: The absolute path to the directory to list.

tools.name: memory
tools.description:
Manage a persistent memory system with three scopes for storing notes and information across conversations.

Memory is organized under /memories/ with three tiers:
- `/memories/` — User memory: persistent notes that survive across all workspaces and conversations. Store preferences, patterns, and general insights here.
- `/memories/session/` — Session memory: notes scoped to the current conversation. Store task-specific context and in-progress notes here. Cleared after the conversation ends.
- `/memories/repo/` — Repository memory: repository-scoped facts stored via Copilot. Only the `create` command is supported for this path.

IMPORTANT: Before creating new memory files, first view the /memories/ directory to understand what already exists. This helps avoid duplicates and maintain organized notes.

Commands:
- `view`: View contents of a file or list directory contents. Can be used on files or directories (e.g., "/memories/" to see all top-level items).
- `create`: Create a new file at the specified path with the given content. Fails if the file already exists.
- `str_replace`: Replace an exact string in a file with a new string. The old_str must appear exactly once in the file.
- `insert`: Insert text at a specific line number in a file. Line 0 inserts at the beginning.
- `delete`: Delete a file or directory (and all its contents).
- `rename`: Rename or move a file or directory from path to new_path. Cannot rename across scopes.

command.description: The operation to perform on the memory file system.

path.description: The absolute path to the file or directory inside /memories/, e.g. "/memories/notes.md". Used by all commands except `rename`.

file_text.description: Required for `create`. The content of the file to create.

old_str.description: Required for `str_replace`. The exact string in the file to replace. Must appear exactly once.

new_str.description: Required for `str_replace`. The new string to replace old_str with.

insert_line.description: Required for `insert`. The 0-based line number to insert text at. 0 inserts before the first line.

insert_text.description: Required for `insert`. The text to insert at the specified line.

view_range.description: Optional for `view`. A two-element array [start_line, end_line] (1-indexed) to view a specific range of lines.

old_path.description: Required for `rename`. The current path of the file or directory to rename.

new_path.description: Required for `rename`. The new path for the file or directory.

tools.name: multi_replace_string_in_file
tools.description: This tool allows you to apply multiple replace_string_in_file operations in a single call, which is more efficient than calling replace_string_in_file multiple times. It takes an array of replacement operations and applies them sequentially. Each replacement operation has the same parameters as replace_string_in_file: filePath, oldString, newString, and explanation. This tool is ideal when you need to make multiple edits across different files or multiple edits in the same file. The tool will provide a summary of successful and failed operations.

explanation.description: A brief explanation of what the multi-replace operation will accomplish.

replacements.description: An array of replacement operations to apply sequentially.

filePath.description: An absolute path to the file to edit.

oldString.description: The exact literal text to replace, preferably unescaped. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string is not the exact literal text or does not match exactly, this replacement will fail.

newString.description: The exact literal text to replace `oldString` with, preferably unescaped. Provide the EXACT text. Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic.

tools.name: read_file
tools.description:
Read the contents of a file.

You must specify the line range you're interested in. Line numbers are 1-indexed. If the file contents returned are insufficient for your task, you may call this tool again to retrieve more content. Prefer reading larger ranges over doing many small reads.

filePath.description: The absolute path of the file to read.

startLine.description: The line number to start reading from, 1-based.

endLine.description: The inclusive line number to end reading at, 1-based.

tools.name: read_notebook_cell_output
tools.description: This tool will retrieve the output for a notebook cell from its most recent execution or restored from disk. The cell may have output even when it has not been run in the current kernel session. This tool has a higher token limit for output length than the runNotebookCell tool.

filePath.description: An absolute path to the notebook file with the cell to run, or the URI of a untitled, not yet named, file, such as `untitled:Untitled-1.ipynb

cellId.description: The ID of the cell for which output should be retrieved.

tools.name: replace_string_in_file
tools.description:
This is a tool for making edits in an existing file in the workspace. For moving or renaming files, use run in terminal tool with the 'mv' command instead. For larger edits, split them into smaller edits and call the edit tool multiple times to ensure accuracy. Before editing, always ensure you have the context to understand the file's contents and context. To edit a file, provide: 1) filePath (absolute path), 2) oldString (MUST be the exact literal text to replace including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc), and 3) newString (MUST be the exact literal text to replace \`oldString\` with (also including all whitespace, indentation, newlines, and surrounding code etc.). Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic.). Each use of this tool replaces exactly ONE occurrence of oldString.

CRITICAL for \`oldString\`: Must uniquely identify the single instance to change. Include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. If this string matches multiple locations, or does not match exactly, the tool will fail. Never use 'Lines 123-456 omitted' from summarized documents or ...existing code... comments in the oldString or newString.

filePath.description: An absolute path to the file to edit.

oldString.description: The exact literal text to replace, preferably unescaped. For single replacements (default), include at least 3 lines of context BEFORE and AFTER the target text, matching whitespace and indentation precisely. For multiple replacements, specify expected_replacements parameter. If this string is not the exact literal text (i.e. you escaped it) or does not match exactly, the tool will fail.

newString.description: The exact literal text to replace `old_string` with, preferably unescaped. Provide the EXACT text. Ensure the resulting code is correct and idiomatic.

tools.name: run_notebook_cell
tools.description: This is a tool for running a code cell in a notebook file directly in the notebook editor. The output from the execution will be returned. Code cells should be run as they are added or edited when working through a problem to bring the kernel state up to date and ensure the code executes successfully. Code cells are ready to run and don't require any pre-processing. If asked to run the first cell in a notebook, you should run the first code cell since markdown cells cannot be executed. NOTE: Avoid executing Markdown cells or providing Markdown cell IDs, as Markdown cells cannot be  executed.

filePath.description: An absolute path to the notebook file with the cell to run, or the URI of a untitled, not yet named, file, such as `untitled:Untitled-1.ipynb

reason.description: An optional explanation of why the cell is being run. This will be shown to the user before the tool is run and is not necessary if it's self-explanatory.

cellId.description: The ID for the code cell to execute. Avoid providing markdown cell IDs as nothing will be executed.

continueOnError.description: Whether or not execution should continue for remaining cells if an error is encountered. Default to false unless instructed otherwise.

tools.name: run_vscode_command
tools.description: Run a command in VS Code. Use this tool to run a command in Visual Studio Code as part of a new workspace creation process only.

commandId.description: The ID of the command to execute. This should be in the format <command>.

name.description: The name of the command to execute. This should be a clear and concise description of the command.

args.description: The arguments to pass to the command. This should be an array of strings.

skipCheck.description: If true, skip checking whether the command exists before executing it.

tools.name: semantic_search
tools.description: Run a natural language search for relevant code or documentation comments from the user's current workspace. Returns relevant code snippets from the user's current workspace if it is large, or the full contents of the workspace if it is small.

query.description: The query to search the codebase for. Should contain all relevant context. Should ideally be text that might appear in the codebase, such as function names, variable names, or comments.

tools.name: test_failure
tools.description: Includes test failure information in the prompt.

tools.name: vscode_askQuestions
tools.description: Use this tool to ask the user a small number of clarifying questions before proceeding. Provide the questions array with concise headers and prompts. Use options for fixed choices, set multiSelect when multiple selections are allowed, and set allowFreeformInput to let users supply their own answer.

questions.description: List of questions to ask the user. Order is preserved.

header.description: Short identifier for the question. Must be unique so answers can be mapped back to the question.

question.description: The question text to display to the user. Keep it concise, ideally one sentence.

multiSelect.description: Allow selecting multiple options when options are provided.

allowFreeformInput.description: Allow freeform text answers in addition to option selection.

options.description: Optional list of selectable answers. If omitted, the question is free text.

label.description: Display label and value for the option.

description.description: Optional secondary text shown with the option.

recommended.description: Mark this option as the recommended default.

tools.name: vscode_listCodeUsages
tools.description:
Find all usages (references, definitions, and implementations) of a code symbol across the workspace. This tool locates where a symbol is referenced, defined, or implemented.

Input:
- "symbol": The exact name of the symbol to search for (function, class, method, variable, type, etc.).
- "uri": A full URI (e.g. "file:///path/to/file.ts") of a file where the symbol appears. Provide either "uri" or "filePath".
- "filePath": A workspace-relative file path (e.g. "src/utils/helpers.ts") of a file where the symbol appears. Provide either "uri" or "filePath".
- "lineContent": A substring of the line of code where the symbol appears. This is used to locate the exact position in the file. Must be the actual text from the file - do NOT fabricate it.

IMPORTANT: The file and line do NOT need to be the definition of the symbol. Any occurrence works - a usage, an import, a call site, etc. You can pick whichever occurrence is most convenient.

If the tool returns an error, retry with corrected input - ensure the file path is correct, the line content matches the actual file content, and the symbol name appears in that line.

Currently supported for: chatagent, css, handlebars, html, instructions, javascript, javascriptreact, less, markdown, prompt, python, scss, skill, typescript, typescriptreact, vue.

symbol.description: The exact name of the symbol (function, class, method, variable, type, etc.) to find usages of.

uri.description: A full URI of a file where the symbol appears (e.g. "file:///path/to/file.ts"). Provide either "uri" or "filePath".

filePath.description: A workspace-relative file path where the symbol appears (e.g. "src/utils/helpers.ts"). Provide either "uri" or "filePath".

lineContent.description: A substring of the line of code where the symbol appears. Used to locate the exact position. Must be actual text from the file.

tools.name: vscode_renameSymbol
tools.description:
Rename a code symbol across the workspace using the language server's rename functionality. This performs a precise, semantics-aware rename that updates all references.

Input:
- "symbol": The exact current name of the symbol to rename.
- "newName": The new name for the symbol.
- "uri": A full URI (e.g. "file:///path/to/file.ts") of a file where the symbol appears. Provide either "uri" or "filePath".
- "filePath": A workspace-relative file path (e.g. "src/utils/helpers.ts") of a file where the symbol appears. Provide either "uri" or "filePath".
- "lineContent": A substring of the line of code where the symbol appears. This is used to locate the exact position in the file. Must be the actual text from the file - do NOT fabricate it.

IMPORTANT: The file and line do NOT need to be the definition of the symbol. Any occurrence works - a usage, an import, a call site, etc. You can pick whichever occurrence is most convenient.

If the tool returns an error, retry with corrected input - ensure the file path is correct, the line content matches the actual file content, and the symbol name appears in that line.

Currently supported for: chatagent, css, handlebars, html, instructions, javascript, javascriptreact, less, markdown, prompt, python, scss, skill, typescript, typescriptreact, vue.

symbol.description: The exact current name of the symbol to rename.

newName.description: The new name for the symbol.

uri.description: A full URI of a file where the symbol appears (e.g. "file:///path/to/file.ts"). Provide either "uri" or "filePath".

filePath.description: A workspace-relative file path where the symbol appears (e.g. "src/utils/helpers.ts"). Provide either "uri" or "filePath".

lineContent.description: A substring of the line of code where the symbol appears. Used to locate the exact position. Must be actual text from the file.

tools.name: vscode_searchExtensions_internal
tools.description: This is a tool for browsing Visual Studio Code Extensions Marketplace. It allows the model to search for extensions and retrieve detailed information about them. The model should use this tool whenever it needs to discover extensions or resolve information about known ones. To use the tool, the model has to provide the category of the extensions, relevant search keywords, or known extension IDs. Note that search results may include false positives, so reviewing and filtering is recommended.

category.description: The category of extensions to search for

ids.description: The ids of the extensions to search for

tools.name: await_terminal
tools.description: Wait for a background terminal command to complete. Returns the output, exit code, or timeout status.

id.description: The ID of the terminal to await (returned by run_in_terminal when isBackground=true).

timeout.description: Timeout in milliseconds. If the command does not complete within this time, returns the output collected so far with a timeout indicator. Use 0 for no timeout.

tools.name: create_and_run_task
tools.description: Creates and runs a build, run, or custom task for the workspace by generating or adding to a tasks.json file based on the project structure (such as package.json or README.md). If the user asks to build, run, launch and they have no tasks.json file, use this tool. If they ask to create or add a task, use this tool.

workspaceFolder.description: The absolute path of the workspace folder where the tasks.json file will be created.

task.description: The task to add to the new tasks.json file.

type.description: The type of the task. The only supported value is 'shell'.

command.description: The shell command to run for the task. Use this to specify commands for building or running the application.

args.description: The arguments to pass to the command.

isBackground.description: Whether the task runs in the background without blocking the UI or other tasks. Set to true for long-running processes like watch tasks or servers that should continue executing without requiring user attention. When false, the task will block the terminal until completion.

problemMatcher.description: The problem matcher to use to parse task output for errors and warnings. Can be a predefined matcher like '$tsc' (TypeScript), '$eslint - stylish', '$gcc', etc., or a custom pattern defined in tasks.json. This helps VS Code display errors in the Problems panel and enables quick navigation to error locations.

group.description: The group to which the task belongs.

tools.name: get_terminal_output
tools.description: Get the output of a terminal command previously started with run_in_terminal

id.description: The ID of the terminal to check.

tools.name: kill_terminal
tools.description: Kill a terminal by its ID. Use this to clean up terminals that are no longer needed (e.g., after stopping a server or when a long-running task completes). The terminal ID is returned by run_in_terminal when isBackground=true.

id.description: The ID of the background terminal to kill (returned by run_in_terminal when isBackground=true).

tools.name: manage_todo_list
tools.description:
Manage a structured todo list to track progress and plan tasks throughout your coding session. Use this tool VERY frequently to ensure task visibility and proper planning.

When to use this tool:
- Complex multi-step work requiring planning and tracking
- When user provides multiple tasks or requests (numbered/comma-separated)
- After receiving new instructions that require multiple steps
- BEFORE starting work on any todo (mark as in-progress)
- IMMEDIATELY after completing each todo (mark completed individually)
- When breaking down larger tasks into smaller actionable steps
- To give users visibility into your progress and planning

When NOT to use:
- Single, trivial tasks that can be completed in one step
- Purely conversational/informational requests
- When just reading files or performing simple searches

CRITICAL workflow:
1. Plan tasks by writing todo list with specific, actionable items
2. Mark ONE todo as in-progress before starting work
3. Complete the work for that specific todo
4. Mark that todo as completed IMMEDIATELY
5. Move to next todo and repeat

Todo states:
- not-started: Todo not yet begun
- in-progress: Currently working (limit ONE at a time)
- completed: Finished successfully

IMPORTANT: Mark todos completed as soon as they are done. Do not batch completions.

todoList.description: Complete array of all todo items. Must include ALL items - both existing and new.

id.description: Unique identifier for the todo. Use sequential numbers starting from 1.

title.description: Concise action-oriented todo label (3-7 words). Displayed in UI.

status.description: not-started: Not begun | in-progress: Currently working (max 1) | completed: Fully finished with no blockers

tools.name: open_browser_page
tools.description: Open a new browser page in the integrated browser at the given URL.

url.description: The URL to open in the browser.

tools.name: run_in_terminal
tools.description:
This tool allows you to execute shell commands in a persistent zsh terminal session, preserving environment variables, working directory, and other context across multiple commands.

Command Execution:
- Use && to chain simple commands on one line
- Prefer pipelines | over temporary files for data flow
- Never create a sub-shell (eg. bash -c "command") unless explicitly asked

Directory Management:
- Prefer relative paths when navigating directories, only use absolute when the path is far away or the current cwd is not expected
- Remember when isBackground=false is specified, that shell and cwd is reused until it is moved to the background
- Use $PWD for current directory references
- Consider using pushd/popd for directory stack management
- Supports directory shortcuts like ~ and -

Program Execution:
- Supports Python, Node.js, and other executables
- Install packages via package managers (brew, apt, etc.)
- Use which or command -v to verify command availability

Background Processes:
- For long-running tasks (e.g., servers), set isBackground=true
- Returns a terminal ID for checking status and runtime later

Output Management:
- Output is automatically truncated if longer than 60KB to prevent context overflow
- Use head, tail, grep, awk to filter and limit output size
- For pager commands, disable paging: git --no-pager or add | cat
- Use wc -l to count lines before displaying large outputs

Best Practices:
- Quote variables: "$var" instead of $var to handle spaces
- Use find with -exec or xargs for file operations
- Be specific with commands to avoid excessive output
- Avoid printing credentials unless absolutely required
- Use type to check command type (builtin, function, alias)
- Use jobs, fg, bg for job control
- Use [[ ]] for conditional tests instead of [ ]
- Prefer $() over backticks for command substitution
- Use setopt errexit for strict error handling
- Take advantage of zsh globbing features (**, extended globs)

command.description: The command to run in the terminal.

explanation.description: A one-sentence description of what the command does. This will be shown to the user before the command is run.

goal.description: A short description of the goal or purpose of the command (e.g., "Install dependencies", "Start development server").

isBackground.description:
Whether the command starts a background process.

- If true, a new shell will be spawned where the cwd is the workspace directory and will run asynchronously in the background and you will not see the output.

- If false, a single shell is shared between all non-background terminals where the cwd starts at the workspace directory and is remembered until that terminal is moved to the background, the tool call will block on the command finishing and only then you will get the output.

Examples of background processes: building in watch mode, starting a server. You can check the output of a background process later on by using get_terminal_output.

timeout.description: An optional timeout in milliseconds. When provided, the tool will stop tracking the command after this duration and return the output collected so far. Be conservative with the timeout duration, give enough time that the command would complete on a low-end machine. Use 0 for no timeout. If it's not clear how long the command will take then use 0 to avoid prematurely terminating it, never guess too low.

tools.name: runSubagent
tools.description:
Launch a new agent to handle complex, multi-step tasks autonomously. This tool is good at researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries, use this agent to perform the search for you.

- Agents do not run async or in the background, you will wait for the agent's result.
- When the agent is done, it will return a single message back to you. The result returned by the agent is not visible to the user. To show the user the result, you should send a text message back to the user with a concise summary of the result.
- Each agent invocation is stateless. You will not be able to send additional messages to the agent, nor will the agent be able to communicate with you outside of its final report. Therefore, your prompt should contain a highly detailed task description for the agent to perform autonomously and you should specify exactly what information the agent should return back to you in its final and only message to you.
- The agent's outputs should generally be trusted
- Clearly tell the agent whether you expect it to write code or just to do research (search, file reads, web fetches, etc.), since it is not aware of the user's intent
- If the user asks for a certain agent, you MUST provide that EXACT agent name (case-sensitive) to invoke that specific agent.

prompt.description: A detailed description of the task for the agent to perform

description.description: A short (3-5 word) description of the task

agentName.description: Optional name of a specific agent to invoke. If not provided, uses the current agent.

tools.name: terminal_last_command
tools.description: Get the last command run in the active terminal.

tools.name: terminal_selection
tools.description: Get the current selection in the active terminal.

tools.name: activate_data_analysis_tools
tools.description:
Call this tool when you need access to a new category of tools. The category of tools is described as follows:

This group of tools is designed to facilitate the analysis of data files, ensuring that users follow best practices throughout the workflow. The 'data_analysis_best_practice' tool serves as a foundational step, guiding users on the recommended procedures for analyzing data files. Once the best practices are established, users can utilize the 'check_panel_open' tool to determine if a 'Data Viewer' panel is currently active, which is essential for subsequent operations. 

The tools also include 'get_table_schema' for retrieving the structure of tabular data, allowing users to understand the data's organization before diving into analysis. This is particularly useful for identifying the types of data present and preparing for more detailed examination. The 'read_rows' tool complements this by enabling users to extract specific rows from the data, either from the active panel or directly from a file, with options for column filtering. 

Finally, the 'export_panel_data' tool allows users to save the current state of the data in the active panel, ensuring that they can preserve their work or prepare data for further analysis in different formats. Together, these tools create a cohesive environment for effective data analysis, from understanding data structure to exporting results.

Be sure to call this tool if you need a capability related to the above.

tools.name: activate_ai_agent_evaluation_tools
tools.description:
Call this tool when you need access to a new category of tools. The category of tools is described as follows:

This group of tools focuses on the evaluation and development of AI agents, providing a structured approach to converting agent specifications into executable code and ensuring that evaluation processes are thorough and effective. The 'evaluation_planner' tool is crucial as it initiates the evaluation process by guiding users through a conversation to clarify essential metrics, datasets, and runtime requirements, ensuring that all necessary details are gathered before proceeding. 

Once the evaluation parameters are established, users can leverage the 'get_agent_model_code_sample' tool to obtain relevant code snippets for AI agent development, facilitating the coding process. Additionally, the 'convert_declarative_agent_to_code' tool assists in transforming declarative specifications into runnable code, providing best practices and examples to streamline this conversion. 

For executing AI applications, the 'evaluation_agent_runner_best_practices' tool offers guidance on using agent runners effectively, ensuring that multiple queries from test datasets are handled appropriately for evaluation purposes. Lastly, the 'get_evaluation_code_gen_best_practices' and 'get_tracing_code_gen_best_practices' tools provide best practices for code generation related to evaluation and tracing, respectively, ensuring that users adhere to industry standards and optimize their AI applications. Together, these tools create a comprehensive framework for developing, evaluating, and refining AI agents.

Be sure to call this tool if you need a capability related to the above.

tools.name: ai-mlstudio-get_trend_data
tools.description: Gets the evaluation trend data from the currently focused Evaluation Trend Analysis view (either standalone panel or embedded trend in Data Viewer). Returns: dataPoints (array of files with fileName, filePath, and metrics - metric values are AVERAGED across selected rows), selectedMetrics (list of metrics being compared), jsonArrayProperty (for JSON files, indicates which array property the data comes from), selectedRows (1-based row indices used for averaging, undefined means all rows), totalRows (total row count). Use this tool when the user asks to analyze or discuss evaluation trends. The tool automatically detects which trend view is currently active/focused.

tools.name: ai-mlstudio-read_cell
tools.description: Prerequisite: ALWAYS call 'data_analysis_best_practice' tool first to understand the analysis workflow before using this tool. Reads a specific cell value from the currently open 'Data Viewer' panel or from a file by row number and column name. If a 'Data Viewer' panel is open (check using 'check_panel_open'), uses data from that panel (faster). Otherwise, reads from the specified filePath (supports CSV, JSONL, and JSON files). For JSON files: if it's a root-level array or AITK Local Evaluation format, the tool auto-selects the appropriate property; otherwise, it returns available array properties and asks you to prompt the user to select one, then call again with selectedJsonProperty. Returns the cell value along with its data type. For object/array values, returns pretty-printed JSON. Important: Row numbers are 1-based (first row is 1, NOT 0). Column name must match exactly.

filePath.description: Optional. The absolute file path to a CSV, JSONL, or JSON file. Not needed if 'Data Viewer' panel is already open.

row.description: The row number (1-based, first row is 1) of the cell to read.

column.description: The column name of the cell to read.

selectedJsonProperty.description: Optional. For JSON files only. The name of the array property to read from. Use this when the tool previously returned available array properties and the user has selected one.

tools.name: aitk-add_agent_debug
tools.description: This tool provides code snippets to enable interactive debugging for agents and workflows in VS Code, fully integrated with AI Toolkit Agent Inspector. Use this to add local testing and debugging capabilities.

tools.name: aitk-agent_as_server
tools.description: This tool provides best practices and steps for wrapping an agent-framework-based agent or workflow to serve as an HTTP server.

tools.name: aitk-gen_windows_ml_web_demo
tools.description: Use this tool to generate Windows ML web demo code for local Windows models. This includes Gradio-style web demo with interactive web interface for models runs locally using windows ML. This tool is only for models running locally on Windows ML. It does not support remote inference or cloud-hosted models. Common trigger phrases include: 'Generate Windows ML web demo for local model [modelId]'; 'Generate Windows ML gradio sample for local model <modelId>';  'Create local model playground code for <modelId>'. This tool takes in these parameters: modelId, modelPaths, modelDir, executionProvider, deviceType, samplePath, isLLM, outputDir. IMPORTANT: 1. This tool needs multi-turn to get necessary parameters. When calling this tool, NEVER guess/ search or infer missing parameter values, because tool itself will handle or re-prompt for missing values. For examples, if user doesn't provide modelPaths or modelDir, do NOT search or select folder and pass in on behalf of user. 2. When user corrects wrong parameter values or provides missing parameter values, update the previous parameter list call this tool again. Do NOT ONLY pass the new parameters, but pass entire updated parameter list.

modelPaths.description: (For non-LLM) A JSON object mapping model names to their ONNX file paths. Keys are model identifiers, values are absolute paths. Example: {'model': 'path/to/model.onnx'} for single model, or {'encoder': 'path/encoder.onnx', 'decoder': 'path/decoder.onnx'} for multiple models.

modelDir.description: (For LLM) Directory containing the ONNX model files.

executionProvider.description: Execution provider used for model inference. e.g., 'CPUExecutionProvider', 'CUDAExecutionProvider', 'DirectMLExecutionProvider'.

deviceType.description: Runtime device type for the model inference. e.g. 'CPU', 'GPU'.

outputDir.description: Path to the directory where output will be saved.

isLLM.description: Indicates whether the model is a Large Language Model (LLM).

confirmed.description: Set to true to confirm regeneration when output directory already exists. Default is false.

tools.name: activate_notebook_kernel_configuration_tools
tools.description:
Call this tool when you need access to a new category of tools. The category of tools is described as follows:

This group of tools is designed to facilitate the configuration and management of notebook environments by allowing users to select and start different types of kernels. The 'configure_python_notebook' tool specifically enables users to select a Python kernel, which is essential for running Python code within a notebook interface. This is particularly useful for data analysis, machine learning, and scientific computing tasks that rely on Python's extensive libraries and frameworks. On the other hand, the 'configure_non_python_notebook' tool allows users to select and start kernels for other programming languages, broadening the scope of the notebook's functionality beyond Python. This is beneficial for users who work with multiple programming languages and need a flexible environment to execute code in various languages seamlessly.

Together, these tools empower users to customize their notebook experience according to their specific programming needs. By selecting the appropriate kernel, users can ensure that their code executes correctly and efficiently, leveraging the unique features and libraries of each programming language. This capability is particularly valuable in educational settings, research, and collaborative projects where different languages may be used for different tasks.

Moreover, these tools can be integrated into a larger workflow where users switch between languages as needed, enhancing productivity and allowing for more complex projects that require multi-language support. For instance, a data scientist might use Python for data manipulation and visualization while also incorporating R for statistical analysis, all within the same notebook environment.

In summary, the notebook kernel configuration tools provide essential functionality for users looking to optimize their coding environment, making it easier to work with various programming languages in a cohesive manner.

Be sure to call this tool if you need a capability related to the above.

tools.name: activate_notebook_package_management
tools.description:
Call this tool when you need access to a new category of tools. The category of tools is described as follows:

This group of tools is designed specifically for managing Python packages within Jupyter notebooks. The 'notebook_install_packages' tool allows users to install a specified list of packages directly into the notebook's kernel, ensuring that the necessary libraries are available for executing Python code cells. This is particularly useful for data analysis, machine learning, and scientific computing tasks where specific packages are required to run the code effectively.

The 'notebook_list_packages' tool complements the installation functionality by providing a way to list all currently installed packages in the selected kernel. This feature is essential for users to verify that the required packages are indeed installed and to check for any version discrepancies that may affect code execution.

Together, these tools streamline the workflow for data scientists and developers working in Jupyter notebooks by simplifying the process of package management. Users can quickly install new libraries as needed and confirm their availability without leaving the notebook environment.

It is important to note that these tools are specifically tailored for use within Jupyter notebooks and are intended for Python code cells only. Users should ensure that they are in the correct environment before utilizing these tools, and if the 'configure_notebooks' tool is available, it should be called prior to using either of these tools to ensure proper configuration.

Overall, this group enhances the usability of Jupyter notebooks by providing essential package management capabilities, allowing users to focus on their coding and analysis tasks without the hassle of manual package management.

Be sure to call this tool if you need a capability related to the above.

tools.name: configure_notebook
tools.description: Tool used to configure a Notebook. ALWAYS use this tool before running/executing any Notebook Cells for the first time or before listing/installing packages in Notebooks for the first time. I.e. there is no need to use this tool more than once for the same notebook.

filePath.description: The absolute path of the notebook with the active kernel.

tools.name: activate_python_environment_tools
tools.description:
Call this tool when you need access to a new category of tools. The category of tools is described as follows:

This group of tools is designed to facilitate the management and retrieval of information regarding Python environments. The tools included are specifically tailored for Python environments such as conda, venv, and other virtual environment managers. They provide essential functionalities that help developers understand and interact with their Python setups effectively.

The first tool, 'get_python_environment_details', allows users to retrieve comprehensive details about the Python environment associated with a specific file or workspace. This includes the type of environment (like conda or venv), the version of Python in use, and a list of all installed packages along with their respective versions. This information is crucial for ensuring that the correct dependencies are in place and for troubleshooting any issues related to package management.

The second tool, 'get_python_executable_details', focuses on providing the necessary details to construct the command for executing Python in the terminal. It helps users avoid common pitfalls by generating the fully qualified path to the Python executable and any required arguments. This is particularly useful for developers who need to run scripts or commands in specific environments without manually figuring out the correct syntax.

Together, these tools streamline the process of managing Python environments, making it easier for developers to configure their setups, verify dependencies, and execute Python commands accurately. They are especially beneficial in collaborative projects where multiple environments may be in use, ensuring consistency and reducing the likelihood of errors.

Overall, this group of tools enhances the developer experience by providing clarity and control over Python environments, which is essential for effective software development and deployment.

Be sure to call this tool if you need a capability related to the above.

tools.name: configure_python_environment
tools.description: This tool configures a Python environment in the given workspace. ALWAYS Use this tool to set up the user's chosen environment and ALWAYS call this tool before using any other Python related tools or running any Python command in the terminal. IMPORTANT: This tool is only for Python environments (venv, virtualenv, conda, pipenv, poetry, pyenv, pixi, or any other Python environment manager). Do not use this tool for npm packages, system packages, Ruby gems, or any other non-Python dependencies.

resourcePath.description: The path to the Python file or workspace for which a Python Environment needs to be configured.

tools.name: install_python_packages
tools.description: Installs Python packages in the given workspace. Use this tool to install Python packages in the user's chosen Python environment. ALWAYS call configure_python_environment before using this tool. IMPORTANT: This tool should only be used to install Python packages using package managers like pip or conda (works with any Python environment: venv, virtualenv, pipenv, poetry, pyenv, pixi, conda, etc.). Do not use this tool to install npm packages, system packages (apt/brew/yum), Ruby gems, or any other non-Python dependencies.

packageList.description: The list of Python packages to install.

resourcePath.description: The path to the Python file or workspace into which the packages are installed. If not provided, the current workspace will be used. Where possible pass the path to the file or workspace.

tools.name: container-tools_get-config
tools.description: Gets the configuration the user has set for the container and container orchestrator (compose) CLIs, including the correct base commands to use and any environment variables that will be set. This tool **MUST** be called before generating or running any container or container orchestrator CLI commands, to ensure the correct base command is used.

tools.name: activate_azure_load_testing_tools
tools.description:
Call this tool when you need access to a new category of tools. The category of tools is described as follows:

This group of tools is designed for managing and executing load tests within the Azure Load Testing environment. The tools facilitate the selection of specific test runs and resources, enabling users to efficiently analyze performance metrics and results. The 'select_azure_load_test_run' tool allows users to choose a particular test run from their Azure Load Testing resources, which is essential for reviewing the outcomes of previous tests and making informed decisions based on performance data. If no specific test run is provided, the tool prompts the user to select from available options, ensuring ease of use and accessibility.

Similarly, the 'select_azure_load_testing_resource' tool enables users to select an Azure Load Testing resource ID. This is crucial for initiating new load tests or analyzing existing ones, as it defines the context in which the tests are executed. Like the previous tool, it prompts users to choose from available resources if none is specified, streamlining the process of resource management.

Together, these tools provide a cohesive workflow for load testing in Azure, allowing users to seamlessly navigate between different test runs and resources. This integration is particularly beneficial for teams that need to conduct multiple tests across various configurations and environments, as it simplifies the selection process and enhances productivity.

By utilizing these tools, users can ensure that they are testing the right applications under the correct conditions, leading to more accurate performance assessments. This is vital for identifying bottlenecks and optimizing application performance in a cloud environment.

Overall, the azure_load_testing_tools group empowers users to effectively manage their load testing efforts in Azure, providing the necessary functionalities to select and analyze test runs and resources with ease.

Be sure to call this tool if you need a capability related to the above.

tools.name: create_load_test_script
tools.description: Generates a load test script based on Locust or JMeter from a URL endpoint or an input file (Postman, Insomnia, or HTTP file). If the user does not provide required parameters (such as endpoint_url or input_file_path), the tool will interactively prompt the user for the missing information during execution, so still you can call this tool to generate script. Use this tool to quickly create a Locust script for load testing.

endpoint_url.description: The target URL endpoint to load test (e.g., https://example.com). If not provided, you can still call this tool to generate a script.

input_file_path.description: The absolute path to the input file (Postman, Insomnia, or HTTP file) for REST APIs. If not provided, you can still call this tool to generate a script.

virtual_user_count.description: The number of virtual users to simulate (e.g., 50). If not provided, you can still call this tool to generate a script.

test_duration_seconds.description: The duration of the test in seconds (e.g., 120). If not provided, you can still call this tool to generate a script.

input_file_type.description: The type of input file (postman, insomnia, http). If not provided, you can still call this tool to generate a script.

testType.description: The type of test to generate (Locust, JMeter). If not provided, you can still call this tool to generate a script.

tools.name: get_azure_load_test_run_insights
tools.description: Retrieves performance and diagnostic insights from a specific Azure Load Testing run using its test run ID and resource ID. If any required parameters are missing, the tool will prompt the user to provide them during execution. Use this tool to analyze test results.

test_run_id.description: The ID of the load test run to fetch insights for. If not provided, you can still call this tool to generate a script.

azure_load_testing_resourceId.description: The full Azure Load Testing resource ID to get insights from. If not provided, you can still call this tool to generate a script.

tools.name: activate_workspace_management_tools
tools.description:
Call this tool when you need access to a new category of tools. The category of tools is described as follows:

This group of tools is focused on managing Postman workspaces, which are essential for organizing API projects and collaboration among team members. The 'getCurrentWorkspace' tool allows users to retrieve information about the current workspace, including its name, ID, and visibility status, providing a quick overview of the workspace context. This is particularly useful for users who need to confirm their current working environment before making changes or executing requests.

The 'switchWorkspace' tool complements this functionality by enabling users to switch between different workspaces seamlessly. By requiring the workspace ID, it allows for quick navigation across various projects or teams, facilitating efficient workflow management. Together, these tools empower users to maintain control over their workspace environment, ensuring they are always working in the correct context.

In practice, users can first utilize 'getCurrentWorkspace' to check their current settings and then use 'switchWorkspace' to navigate to a different workspace as needed. This is especially beneficial in collaborative settings where multiple projects are being handled simultaneously.

Overall, these tools enhance the user experience by providing essential workspace management capabilities, making it easier to organize and switch between different API projects within Postman.

Be sure to call this tool if you need a capability related to the above.

tools.name: activate_api_request_tools
tools.description:
Call this tool when you need access to a new category of tools. The category of tools is described as follows:

This group of tools is designed for managing and executing API requests within the Postman environment. The 'openRequest' tool allows users to open a new request tab in the current workspace, providing a dedicated space for crafting and modifying HTTP requests. This is crucial for developers and testers who need to interact with APIs frequently, as it streamlines the process of creating and organizing requests.

The 'sendRequest' tool is integral to this group, enabling users to execute the currently active HTTP request and retrieve the response. This functionality is essential for testing APIs, as it allows users to validate endpoints, check responses, and debug issues in real-time. By using these tools together, users can efficiently create, modify, and test API requests within a single workflow.

In a typical workflow, a user would first utilize 'openRequest' to set up a new request, input the necessary parameters, headers, and body, and then use 'sendRequest' to execute the request and analyze the response. This iterative process is vital for API development and testing, allowing for rapid feedback and adjustments.

Overall, these tools provide a robust framework for API interaction, making it easier for developers to manage their requests and ensure that their APIs function as intended.

Be sure to call this tool if you need a capability related to the above.

tools.name: getSelectedEnvironment
tools.description: Get information about the currently selected environment including name and ID. No parameters required.

tools.name: activate_cpp_symbol_analysis_tools
tools.description:
Call this tool when you need access to a new category of tools. The category of tools is described as follows:

This group of tools is designed specifically for analyzing and navigating C/C++ codebases, focusing on symbol information and references. The 'GetSymbolInfo_CppTools' tool allows developers to retrieve detailed information about a specific C/C++ symbol, including its definition location, type information, and memory layout details for classes and structs. This is particularly useful for understanding the structure and characteristics of symbols within the code, enabling developers to make informed decisions during development and debugging processes.

On the other hand, 'GetSymbolReferences_CppTools' complements this functionality by providing a means to locate every reference, call site, or usage of a specified C/C++ symbol. Unlike traditional text-based search methods such as grep, this tool offers a more precise and reliable way to track symbol usage across the codebase. This is especially beneficial during refactoring tasks, such as renaming symbols or changing function signatures, where understanding all usages of a symbol is crucial to avoid introducing errors.

Together, these tools enhance the developer's ability to navigate complex C/C++ codebases efficiently. By using 'GetSymbolInfo_CppTools' to gather detailed insights about symbols and 'GetSymbolReferences_CppTools' to track their usage, developers can ensure that their modifications are safe and that they maintain the integrity of the code.

These tools are essential for any C/C++ developer looking to improve their code comprehension and maintainability. They facilitate a deeper understanding of the code structure and promote best practices in code refactoring and maintenance, ultimately leading to higher quality software development.

In summary, this group of tools serves as a powerful resource for C/C++ developers, enabling them to analyze symbols effectively and manage their codebases with confidence.

Be sure to call this tool if you need a capability related to the above.

tools.name: GetSymbolCallHierarchy_CppTools
tools.description: Use GetSymbolCallHierarchy_CppTools to analyze function call relationships for a specific C/C++ function. This tool is especially useful when changing function signatures. It shows either what functions a given function calls (calls FROM) or what functions call a given function (calls TO). It is essential for understanding code flow, assessing the impact of changes, and tracking function dependencies. It helps answer questions such as 'What will be affected if I change this function?' or 'What functions does this call?'.

functionName.description: REQUIRED: The function name. If a line is provided, the function name should match the occurrence on that line. Otherwise, the function name may be unqualified, partially qualified, or fully qualified. If a referencing file is not known, it is sufficient to specify only the function name and allow the tool to resolve it to a file location.

callsFrom.description: REQUIRED: A boolean indicating whether to include calls from the specified function or calls to the specified function. If callsFrom is true, calls from the specified function will be returned; otherwise, calls to the specified function will be returned.

filePath.description: OPTIONAL: A path to the file containing the specified symbol name, or otherwise contextual to the current request. Absolute paths are strongly preferred. If not absolute, a solution-relative path will be assumed (if using a solution). Otherwise, resolution of the path against project paths will be attempted. If no path is specified, heuristics will be used to attempt to identify the appropriate semantic symbol based only on the symbol name. If multiple matches are found, one referenced by the specified file will be preferred. If there is an active/specific file available, provide it. Otherwise, allow the tool to resolve the symbol name to a file location.

line.description: OPTIONAL: The line number of the specified symbol name in the specified file. This should be a 1-based line index, not a 0-based line index. If no file is provided, the line is not used and may be omitted. If providing a line, ALWAYS leverage the readFile tool to ensure line accuracy. If a line number is not known, resolve the symbol to a file location.

tools.name: activate_azure_management_tools
tools.description:
Call this tool when you need access to a new category of tools. The category of tools is described as follows:

This group encompasses a wide range of tools designed for managing various Azure resources and services. The tools facilitate operations related to Azure Container Registry, Kubernetes Service, App Configuration, Application Insights, App Service, and more. They enable users to perform tasks such as managing container images, querying Kubernetes cluster metadata, and configuring application settings, all of which are essential for cloud application development and deployment.

Additionally, the tools support infrastructure as code (IaC) practices through Bicep schema operations, allowing users to automate resource deployment and management. The Azure Developer CLI (azd) further streamlines the development workflow by providing commands for project initialization, resource provisioning, and monitoring, making it easier to build and manage applications on Azure.

The group also includes tools for monitoring and logging, such as Azure Monitor and Datadog, which help users track the health and performance of their Azure resources. Furthermore, tools for managing databases (Cosmos DB, MySQL, PostgreSQL), messaging (Service Bus, Event Hubs), and storage (Azure Storage) are included, providing comprehensive capabilities for data management and communication.

Security and access management are addressed through tools for managing Azure Key Vault and Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), ensuring that users can securely manage sensitive information and permissions. Overall, these tools work together to provide a robust framework for managing Azure resources efficiently and effectively, supporting a wide range of cloud-based applications and services.

Be sure to call this tool if you need a capability related to the above.

tools.name: activate_azure_cli_tools
tools.description:
Call this tool when you need access to a new category of tools. The category of tools is described as follows:

This group consists of tools specifically designed to enhance the user experience with Azure Command-Line Interface (CLI) tools. The first tool focuses on generating Azure CLI commands based on user-defined goals, allowing users to easily accomplish tasks without needing to remember the exact syntax or parameters required for each command. This feature is particularly useful for users who may be new to Azure CLI or those who want to streamline their command-line operations.

The second tool provides installation instructions for various Azure CLI tools, including the Azure CLI (az), Azure Developer CLI (azd), and Azure Functions Core Tools CLI (func). This ensures that users can quickly set up the necessary tools to interact with Azure services from the command line, facilitating a smoother onboarding process and reducing barriers to entry for new users.

Together, these tools empower users to effectively utilize Azure CLI for managing resources, automating tasks, and integrating Azure services into their workflows. By simplifying command generation and installation, this group enhances productivity and encourages best practices in cloud resource management.

Be sure to call this tool if you need a capability related to the above.

tools.name: activate_azure_resource_management
tools.description:
Call this tool when you need access to a new category of tools. The category of tools is described as follows:

This group includes tools focused on managing Azure subscriptions and resource groups, which are fundamental components of Azure's resource organization and governance. The first tool allows users to list all resource groups within a specified subscription, providing essential information such as resource group names and IDs. This capability is crucial for users who need to understand their resource organization and manage resources effectively.

The second tool enables users to list all or current subscriptions associated with their Azure account. It returns key details such as subscription IDs, display names, states, and tenant IDs, which are vital for governance, policy enforcement, access management, and cost management. By having visibility into their subscriptions, users can make informed decisions regarding resource allocation and management.

Together, these tools facilitate effective resource governance and management in Azure, allowing users to maintain control over their cloud resources and ensure compliance with organizational policies. They serve as foundational tools for users looking to optimize their Azure environment and manage resources efficiently.

Be sure to call this tool if you need a capability related to the above.

tools.name: mcp_azure_mcp_applens
tools.description:
AppLens diagnostic operations - **Primary tool for diagnosing Azure resource issues and troubleshooting problems**. Use this tool when users ask to:
- Diagnose issues, problems, or errors with Azure resources
- Troubleshoot performance, availability, or reliability problems
- Investigate resource health concerns or unexpected behavior
- Find root causes of application slowness, downtime, or failures
- Get recommendations for fixing Azure resource issues
- Analyze resource problems and get actionable solutions

Always use this tool if user asks to use App Lens in regards to their resource.

This tool provides conversational AI-powered diagnostics that automatically detect issues, identify root causes, and suggest specific remediation steps. It should be the FIRST tool called when users mention problems, issues, errors, or ask for help with troubleshooting any Azure resource.This tool is a hierarchical MCP command router.
Sub commands are routed to MCP servers that require specific fields inside the "parameters" object.
To invoke a command, set "command" and wrap its args in "parameters".
Set "learn=true" to discover available sub commands.

intent.description: The intent of the azure operation to perform.

command.description: The command to execute against the specified tool.

parameters.description: The parameters to pass to the tool command.

learn.description: To learn about the tool and its supported child tools and parameters.

tools.name: mcp_azure_mcp_extension_azqr
tools.description:
Runs Azure Quick Review CLI (azqr) commands to generate compliance/security reports for Azure resources.
This tool should be used when the user wants to identify any non-compliant configurations or areas for improvement in their Azure resources.
Requires a subscription id and optionally a resource group name. Returns the generated report file's path.
Note that Azure Quick Review CLI (azqr) is different from Azure CLI (az).

resource-group.description: The name of the Azure resource group. This is a logical container for Azure resources.

tools.name: mcp_azure_mcp_speech
tools.description:
Speech operations - Commands for Azure AI Services Speech functionality including speech-to-text (STT) 
recognition, audio processing, and language detection. Use this tool when you need to convert spoken 
audio to text, process audio files, or work with speech recognition services. This tool supports 
multiple audio formats, configurable recognition languages, profanity filtering options, and both 
simple and detailed output formats. This tool is a hierarchical MCP command router where sub-commands 
are routed to MCP servers that require specific fields inside the "parameters" object. To invoke a 
command, set "command" and wrap its arguments in "parameters". Set "learn=true" to discover available 
sub-commands for different Azure AI Services Speech operations. Note that this tool requires Azure AI 
Services Speech endpoints and will only access speech resources accessible to the authenticated user.This tool is a hierarchical MCP command router.

tools.name: get_python_executable_details
tools.description: This tool will retrieve the details of the Python Environment for the specified file or workspace. ALWAYS use this tool before executing any Python command in the terminal. This tool returns the details of how to construct the fully qualified path and or command including details such as arguments required to run Python in a terminal. Note: Instead of executing `python --version` or `python -c 'import sys; print(sys.executable)'`, use this tool to get the Python executable path to replace the `python` command. E.g. instead of using `python -c 'import sys; print(sys.executable)'`, use this tool to build the command `conda run -n <env_name> -c 'import sys; print(sys.executable)'`. ALWAYS call configure_python_environment before using this tool. IMPORTANT: This tool is only for Python environments (venv, virtualenv, conda, pipenv, poetry, pyenv, pixi, or any other Python environment manager). Do not use this tool for npm packages, system packages, Ruby gems, or any other non-Python dependencies.

resourcePath.description: The path to the Python file or workspace to get the executable information for. If not provided, the current workspace will be used. Where possible pass the path to the file or workspace.

tools.name: get_python_environment_details
tools.description: This tool will retrieve the details of the Python Environment for the specified file or workspace. The details returned include the 1. Type of Python Environment (conda, venv, etc), 2. Version of Python, 3. List of all installed Python packages with their versions. ALWAYS call configure_python_environment before using this tool. IMPORTANT: This tool is only for Python environments (venv, virtualenv, conda, pipenv, poetry, pyenv, pixi, or any other Python environment manager). Do not use this tool for npm packages, system packages, Ruby gems, or any other non-Python dependencies.

resourcePath.description: The path to the Python file or workspace to get the environment information for.
